purpose: Diagnose the nature of a credit-equity stress episode by observing the direction of lead-lag transmission, enabling appropriate portfolio positioning and risk management responses.
components: Credit leads equity by 2-6 months: fundamental/credit-cycle-driven deterioration (2007-08 template). Deploy defensive credit positioning first, expect equity to follow.
Equity leads or moves simultaneously with credit: sentiment/exogenous shock (2020, 2018 Q4 template). Credit stress may be transient and flow-driven rather than fundamental.
Direction of lead-lag is diagnostic of shock type and therefore predictive of episode severity and duration.
Monitor for synthetic credit reverse causality (CDX/ETF flows) that contaminates the signal at high frequencies.
current_assessment: Current HY OAS at 350-420bp shows no credit stress signal. Credit impulse has decelerated from 2023-2024 peak while equities expanded — a divergent signal. The lead-lag diagnostic is not yet activated because neither market is in stress. If credit begins to widen, the framework becomes actionable.
limitations: Passive/ETF flows may be compressing lead time toward zero, reducing actionability
Synthetic credit reverse causality contaminates the high-frequency signal
Lead-lag pattern based on 5-6 episodes — limited sample
Private credit opacity may delay or mask the credit signal in the sequence
2020 episode (simultaneous) may represent new microstructure normal rather than exogenous-shock exception

purpose: Distinguish in real-time between contained credit-equity episodes (1998, 2015-16 template: -22%, 3-6 months) and systemic episodes (1990, 2007-08 template: -38%, 18-30 months) by monitoring banking channel activation indicators.
components: Primary signpost: regional bank equities falling in sympathy with HY spread widening activates the systemic template
Bank CDS widening concurrent with HY widening indicates banking channel engagement
HY sector breadth: narrow (few sectors) vs broad widening
IG crossover: stress migrating from HY to IG indicates fundamental deterioration
MOVE index: rates volatility spilling into credit markets
FRA-OIS: funding stress in interbank markets
current_assessment: CRE exposure at regional banks (28-30% concentration, office down 25-40%) constitutes potential banking channel loading. However, KRE persistently shorted since March 2023 and CMBS BBB- at 800-1200bp suggest this is priced consensus. CET1 ratios at 13-14% provide more buffer than historical episodes. Japan model slow bleed more probable than sudden cascade. Banking channel not currently activated despite CRE stress.
limitations: Based on limited historical episodes (primarily 1990 and 2007-08 for systemic)
13-14% CET1 ratios may change the dynamics vs historical episodes
Regulatory forbearance incentives may prevent activation even with significant losses
Does not account for private credit channels that bypass banks entirely
Detection may not be early enough to be actionable for portfolio positioning

purpose: Assess the current phase of the credit cycle using Minsky financial instability hypothesis integrated with Kalecki-Levy profit identity, identifying whether the system is in hedge, speculative, or Ponzi financing phase and how the fiscal buffer affects transition timing.
components: Kalecki-Levy profit identity: fiscal deficit → corporate profit support → credit quality maintenance
Minsky phase classification: hedge (income covers all obligations), speculative (income covers interest but not principal), Ponzi (income covers neither)
Aggregate vs distributional: aggregate private sector surplus may mask bottom-50% deficit
Compositional fallacy: multiple sectors at Ponzi stage while aggregate appears speculative
Fiscal consolidation trigger: >2pp GDP consolidation triggers credit cycle downshift within 4-6 quarters
Higher pain threshold paradox: fiscal buffer delays adjustment but increases eventual magnitude
current_assessment: The system is in Minsky speculative phase: surface stability concealing growing fragility. The 6-7% fiscal deficit mechanically sustains profits, keeping distance-to-default artificially wide and spreads artificially tight. The artificial decorrelation between credit spreads and equity risk premia is a speculative-phase signal. The maturity wall ($900B-$1.1T, 2025-2027) represents the micro-level stress that may overwhelm the macro-level Kalecki support, particularly for lower HY/leveraged loans.
limitations: Empirically untested whether the Kalecki floor can be overwhelmed by firm-level maturity wall stress
No clear threshold for when distributional stress overwhelms aggregate support
Minsky phases are qualitative classifications with subjective boundaries
The framework does not specify timing — could persist in speculative phase for years
Fiscal consolidation trigger (2pp GDP) is approximate and politically contingent

purpose: Estimate the probability that a multi-step credit-equity contagion chain will complete vs being interrupted by policy intervention or self-correction, providing a counterweight to narrative-driven tail risk analysis.
components: Count the number of discrete steps required for full contagion
Apply base rate of ~38% median completion from 6 historical episodes
Policy intervention is the primary chain-breaker — assess current policy space
Each additional step reduces completion probability multiplicatively
Private credit spillover chain requires 6 steps suggesting 15-25% completion probability
CRE-bank cascade joint probability estimated at 3-8%
current_assessment: Most contagion chains under discussion require 4-6 steps. Base rates suggest 15-38% completion probability. Fed retains significant policy space (modified Taylor Rule credit term suggests willingness to respond). However, inflation constraint could block the policy response, significantly increasing completion probability. The framework suggests monitoring rather than base-case positioning for most multi-step chains.
limitations: Based on only 6 episodes — too few for statistical inference
±10pp uncertainty on specific probabilities
Excludes structural changes in market plumbing (CLOs, ETFs, private credit) that may change completion rates
Policy intervention assumption requires the Fed not being constrained by inflation
Does not account for novel contagion channels with no historical precedent

purpose: Estimate the probability of disorderly repricing in opaque credit markets by applying historical base rates from analogous opaque sectors, given the absence of direct precedent for private credit at $1.7T scale.
components: Five historical analogues: S&Ls (1980s), SIVs (2007-08), junk bonds (1989-90), Japanese jusen (1990s), AT1 CoCos (2023)
Base rate outcome: 60-80% experienced disorderly repricing
Median lag from opacity to repricing: 4-5 years
Three spillover channels: semi-liquid vehicle redemptions (~$300B BDCs/interval funds), insurance RBC rebalancing (~$700B exposure), information contagion from first forced mark-to-market
Observable leading indicator: PIK toggle usage (currently 5-8%, up from ~2% in 2022)
current_assessment: Private credit at $1.7T is outside all public indices and financial conditions measures. PIK toggle usage trending up. Mark-to-recovery gap unknown (5-25% range). Base rate suggests eventual disorderly repricing is more likely than not, but median lag of 4-5 years means timing is highly uncertain. This is the highest-importance, lowest-precision risk in credit-equity linkage.
limitations: All five analogues are structurally dissimilar to current private credit
60-80% repricing rate from 5 cases is not statistically robust
Median lag of 4-5 years is operationally unhelpful
Mark-to-recovery gap range of 5-25% is too wide to be actionable
The analogues predate current regulatory frameworks and market structures
